CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from motivation to method and experiments; the main contributions are stated early and the methodology (three sememe-encoded models SSA, SAC, SAT) is described clearly. Notation appears consistent and well-defined in the summary, and figures/tables (e.g., results on word similarity and analogy tasks) are informative; writing quality is good. Minor presentation issues remain: the treatment of sememe hierarchical structure is deferred to limitations rather than integrated into methodological discussion, and the absence of a code release is not addressed in the clarity-oriented sections, which would aid reproducibility and reader comprehension. | B(4): The paper is overall well-organized with a clear progression from motivation through method (SSA, SAC, SAT) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and methodology are generally presented clearly—hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is thorough—though the summary suggests occasional simplifications (e.g., treating sememe sets as unordered) that might benefit from a brief clarifying remark in the methods section. Figures, tables and examples are reported as effective for the evaluations used, and the writing quality is good with minimal ambiguity or grammatical issues. As a readability-focused reviewer, I find it well-written with only minor presentation refinements needed.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from motivation to method and experiments; the main contributions are stated early and the methodology (three sememe-encoded models SSA, SAC, SAT) is described clearly. Minor presentation issues remain: the treatment of sememe hierarchical structure is deferred to limitations rather than integrated into methodological discussion, and the absence of a code release is not addressed in the clarity-oriented sections, which would aid reproducibility and reader comprehension.

As a readability-focused reviewer, I find it well-written with only minor presentation refinements needed.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This work sits squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML venue: it proposes novel adaptations to a canonical representation-learning framework (Skip-gram) using linguistically motivated sememe annotations (HowNet), and evaluates on standard lexical tasks (similarity, analogy) against appropriate baselines. The audience (representation learning and lexical semantics) will find the contribution relevant, and the methodology and evaluation metrics meet venue expectations. My remaining reservations are practical: the approach depends on a language-specific resource (HowNet) and the summary does not state code release, which may limit reproducibility and broader impact; these are minor scope concerns rather than reasons to reject. | B(4): This paper addresses a core NLP representation learning problem by integrating HowNet sememe annotations into Skip-gram and proposing attention-based sememe selection models (SAC/SAT), which is directly relevant to top-tier NLP/ML audiences. The methodology and evaluations (word similarity, analogy, WSD-style tests) follow community standards and show empirical gains, but the work’s dependence on HowNet and the simplified unordered-sememe assumption limit generality and cross-lingual impact—minor scope concerns that keep it from being a perfect fit.

reviewer_a.justification: This work sits squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML venue: it proposes novel adaptations to a canonical representation-learning framework (Skip-gram) using linguistically motivated sememe annotations (HowNet), and evaluates on standard lexical tasks (similarity, analogy) against appropriate baselines. The audience (representation learning and lexical semantics) will find the contribution relevant, and the methodology and evaluation metrics meet venue expectations. My remaining reservations are practical: the approach depends on a language-specific resource (HowNet) and the summary does not state code release, which may limit reproducibility and broader impact; these are minor scope concerns rather than reasons to reject.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP representation learning problem by integrating HowNet sememe annotations into Skip-gram and proposing attention-based sememe selection models (SAC/SAT), which is directly relevant to top-tier NLP/ML audiences. The methodology and evaluations (word similarity, analogy, WSD-style tests) follow community standards and show empirical gains, but the work’s dependence on HowNet and the simplified unordered-sememe assumption limit generality and cross-lingual impact—minor scope concerns that keep it from being a perfect fit.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): From the provided summary, the paper contributes a modestly novel combination: integrating HowNet sememe annotations into the Skip-gram framework and jointly learning sememe, sense and word embeddings, with attention-based sense/sememe selection (SAC/SAT). These ideas are a non-trivial application of existing components (Skip-gram + attention) rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, and closely related prior work already models sememe information (e.g., sememe prediction and semantic regularization). The explicit joint learning of sememes/senses/words and the attention-driven disambiguation provide useful, concrete novelty, but overall the contribution reads as an incremental advance rather than a major conceptual breakthrough. | B(3): The paper proposes a reasonably novel integration of HowNet sememes into Skip-gram and, as claimed, jointly learns sememe, sense and word embeddings with attention-based selection—an extension beyond prior sememe-prediction or sememe-regularization work (e.g., sememe prediction via embeddings). However, the core formulation is an incremental adaptation of existing skip-gram/sense-aware embedding ideas and sememe modeling, rather than a fundamentally new paradigm; the attention-based sememe selection and joint representation learning are useful but non-radical technical advances that an expert would view as a moderate novelty.

reviewer_a.justification: From the provided summary, the paper contributes a modestly novel combination: integrating HowNet sememe annotations into the Skip-gram framework and jointly learning sememe, sense and word embeddings, with attention-based sense/sememe selection (SAC/SAT). These ideas are a non-trivial application of existing components (Skip-gram + attention) rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, and closely related prior work already models sememe information (e.g., sememe prediction and semantic regularization). The explicit joint learning of sememes/senses/words and the attention-driven disambiguation provide useful, concrete novelty, but overall the contribution reads as an incremental advance rather than a major conceptual breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a reasonably novel integration of HowNet sememes into Skip-gram and, as claimed, jointly learns sememe, sense and word embeddings with attention-based selection—an extension beyond prior sememe-prediction or sememe-regularization work (e.g., sememe prediction via embeddings). However, the core formulation is an incremental adaptation of existing skip-gram/sense-aware embedding ideas and sememe modeling, rather than a fundamentally new paradigm; the attention-based sememe selection and joint representation learning are useful but non-radical technical advances that an expert would view as a moderate novelty.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles a meaningful problem—incorporating sememe-level lexical knowledge (HowNet) into word/sense embeddings—and demonstrates measurable gains on word similarity and analogy tasks using three sememe-encoded Skip-gram variants (SSA, SAC, SAT). From my reproducibility-focused perspective, the approach is practically useful and offers interpretable attention-based sememe selection, but its impact is constrained by reliance on HowNet (language/resource-specific), omission of sememe relational structure, and no mention of code release; thus it is a solid, moderately impactful contribution likely to be built on within its niche rather than transform the broader field. | B(3): This paper tackles an important problem—incorporating explicitly annotated semantic primitives (HowNet sememes) into distributional learning—to improve interpretability and sense-aware embeddings. The results showing improved word similarity/analogy and attention-based sememe selection for contextual sense disambiguation indicate useful gains and clearer explanations of sense composition. However, reliance on HowNet (language/domain coverage) and simplifying assumptions (unordered sememe sets, ignoring sememe relations) limit broad applicability, so the work is a solid, useful step rather than a field-changing advance. It is likely to prompt follow-up research integrating richer sememe structure and cross-lingual resources.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a meaningful problem—incorporating sememe-level lexical knowledge (HowNet) into word/sense embeddings—and demonstrates measurable gains on word similarity and analogy tasks using three sememe-encoded Skip-gram variants (SSA, SAC, SAT). From my reproducibility-focused perspective, the approach is practically useful and offers interpretable attention-based sememe selection, but its impact is constrained by reliance on HowNet (language/resource-specific), omission of sememe relational structure, and no mention of code release; thus it is a solid, moderately impactful contribution likely to be built on within its niche rather than transform the broader field.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important problem—incorporating explicitly annotated semantic primitives (HowNet sememes) into distributional learning—to improve interpretability and sense-aware embeddings. The results showing improved word similarity/analogy and attention-based sememe selection for contextual sense disambiguation indicate useful gains and clearer explanations of sense composition. However, reliance on HowNet (language/domain coverage) and simplifying assumptions (unordered sememe sets, ignoring sememe relations) limit broad applicability, so the work is a solid, useful step rather than a field-changing advance. It is likely to prompt follow-up research integrating richer sememe structure and cross-lingual resources.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its claims and provides experimental evidence (word similarity and analogy improvements) using HowNet and reasonable baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST), and it presents three model variants that function as controlled ablations of sememe incorporation. Mathematical framing (extension of Skip-gram) appears internally consistent and hyperparameters are reported, but the summary lacks mention of statistical significance testing and released code/data to fully ensure reproducibility. The authors also admit the simplifying assumption of treating sememe sets as unordered and ignore sememe relations, which is a logical limitation that weakens some generality claims but does not invalidate the reported improvements. Overall the experiments are sound with minor gaps in reproducibility and statistical validation that should be addressed. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are generally supported by experiments showing improved similarity and analogy performance when incorporating HowNet sememes and by attention mechanisms for sense selection; the methodology (extending Skip-gram to jointly learn sememe/sense/word embeddings and comparing to standard baselines) is technically reasonable. Mathematical development appears consistent with the Skip-gram framework as summarized, though no detailed proofs were required. However, there are some technical gaps: evaluation is limited (only Chinese datasets reported), it’s unclear whether statistical significance tests were applied, and the claimed interpretability of attention-based sememe selection is asserted but not rigorously validated. Overall the results justify the conclusions with these minor methodological limitations.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its claims and provides experimental evidence (word similarity and analogy improvements) using HowNet and reasonable baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST), and it presents three model variants that function as controlled ablations of sememe incorporation. Mathematical framing (extension of Skip-gram) appears internally consistent and hyperparameters are reported, but the summary lacks mention of statistical significance testing and released code/data to fully ensure reproducibility. The authors also admit the simplifying assumption of treating sememe sets as unordered and ignore sememe relations, which is a logical limitation that weakens some generality claims but does not invalidate the reported improvements. Overall the experiments are sound with minor gaps in reproducibility and statistical validation that should be addressed.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are generally supported by experiments showing improved similarity and analogy performance when incorporating HowNet sememes and by attention mechanisms for sense selection; the methodology (extending Skip-gram to jointly learn sememe/sense/word embeddings and comparing to standard baselines) is technically reasonable. Mathematical development appears consistent with the Skip-gram framework as summarized, though no detailed proofs were required. However, there are some technical gaps: evaluation is limited (only Chinese datasets reported), it’s unclear whether statistical significance tests were applied, and the claimed interpretability of attention-based sememe selection is asserted but not rigorously validated. Overall the results justify the conclusions with these minor methodological limitations.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against standard and recent embedding baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe) and a sememe-aware prior (MST), uses the same dataset (HowNet/Chinese analogy) and standard metrics (Spearman, accuracy, mean rank), and reports hyperparameters—so overall comparisons are good and largely fair. The authors present multiple ablations (SSA, SAC, SAT) which helps isolate the contribution of attention and sememe integration, and they acknowledge modeling limitations. However, the evaluation omits discussion of some closely related sememe-oriented approaches (e.g., models that predict or regularize sememes via matrix factorization) and it is not explicit whether all baselines were tuned/trained under identical conditions (training corpora and optimization details), so I deduct one point for these minor but important reproducibility/completeness gaps. | B(4): The paper compares against standard and strong embedding baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe) and a task-relevant prior (MST), and evaluations appear to use the same data and metrics (word similarity, analogy) so comparisons are broadly fair. However, it omits direct empirical comparison to other sememe-focused methods (e.g., sememe prediction or matrix-factorization approaches that also incorporate HowNet), which are closely related conceptually and would more directly test the benefit of joint sememe/sense learning. The manuscript documents model differences (SSA/SAC/SAT) and reports improved scores, and the related-work section is reportedly thorough, but the lack of head-to-head results versus other sememe-based work is a notable gap. Given these points I judge the comparisons good but with minor omissions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against standard and recent embedding baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe) and a sememe-aware prior (MST), uses the same dataset (HowNet/Chinese analogy) and standard metrics (Spearman, accuracy, mean rank), and reports hyperparameters—so overall comparisons are good and largely fair. The authors present multiple ablations (SSA, SAC, SAT) which helps isolate the contribution of attention and sememe integration, and they acknowledge modeling limitations. However, the evaluation omits discussion of some closely related sememe-oriented approaches (e.g., models that predict or regularize sememes via matrix factorization) and it is not explicit whether all baselines were tuned/trained under identical conditions (training corpora and optimization details), so I deduct one point for these minor but important reproducibility/completeness gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against standard and strong embedding baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe) and a task-relevant prior (MST), and evaluations appear to use the same data and metrics (word similarity, analogy) so comparisons are broadly fair. However, it omits direct empirical comparison to other sememe-focused methods (e.g., sememe prediction or matrix-factorization approaches that also incorporate HowNet), which are closely related conceptually and would more directly test the benefit of joint sememe/sense learning. The manuscript documents model differences (SSA/SAC/SAT) and reports improved scores, and the related-work section is reportedly thorough, but the lack of head-to-head results versus other sememe-based work is a notable gap. Given these points I judge the comparisons good but with minor omissions.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs multiple standard evaluations (word similarity and analogy), compares against appropriate baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST), reports hyperparameters, and explicitly tests three sememe-encoding variants (SSA, SAC, SAT), which provides solid empirical support for the main claims. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and the authors discuss limitations (ignoring sememe relational/hierarchical structure). Missing for full reproducibility and deeper attribution are a public code/data release and clearer ablations isolating contributions of individual components (e.g., impact of attention vs. sememe supervision, different negative sampling/initialization regimes), so the evaluation is strong but has minor gaps that prevent it being fully comprehensive. | B(4): The experiments convincingly demonstrate benefits of injecting HowNet sememes (improvements on word similarity and analogy, comparison to standard baselines and MST) and the three proposed models (SSA, SAC, SAT) provide useful ablation over aggregation vs attention. Related work is treated carefully and the paper situates itself relative to other sememe-oriented methods (e.g., sememe prediction / matrix-factorization approaches). The paper also acknowledges key limitations (ignoring sememe relational structure) and reports hyperparameters, but it lacks clearer reproduction artefacts (no code mentioned) and deeper sensitivity analyses (e.g., robustness to HowNet coverage, effect of sememe noise, or more fine-grained ablations), which prevents an "extremely thorough" rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs multiple standard evaluations (word similarity and analogy), compares against appropriate baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST), reports hyperparameters, and explicitly tests three sememe-encoding variants (SSA, SAC, SAT), which provides solid empirical support for the main claims. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and the authors discuss limitations (ignoring sememe relational/hierarchical structure). Missing for full reproducibility and deeper attribution are a public code/data release and clearer ablations isolating contributions of individual components (e.g., impact of attention vs. sememe supervision, different negative sampling/initialization regimes), so the evaluation is strong but has minor gaps that prevent it being fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments convincingly demonstrate benefits of injecting HowNet sememes (improvements on word similarity and analogy, comparison to standard baselines and MST) and the three proposed models (SSA, SAC, SAT) provide useful ablation over aggregation vs attention. Related work is treated carefully and the paper situates itself relative to other sememe-oriented methods (e.g., sememe prediction / matrix-factorization approaches). The paper also acknowledges key limitations (ignoring sememe relational structure) and reports hyperparameters, but it lacks clearer reproduction artefacts (no code mentioned) and deeper sensitivity analyses (e.g., robustness to HowNet coverage, effect of sememe noise, or more fine-grained ablations), which prevents an "extremely thorough" rating.

main_claims: Jointly learns representations of sememes, senses and words using HowNet annotations for the first time (as claimed).
Incorporating sememe information via sememe-encoded models (SSA, SAC, SAT) improves word representation learning and word sense disambiguation.
Attention-based sememe selection (SAC/SAT) can select appropriate senses/sememes in context, yielding better embeddings and explainability.
The proposed SE-WRL models significantly outperform conventional baselines on word similarity and word analogy tasks.
_summary.methodology: Extend the Skip-gram framework to incorporate HowNet sememe annotations, proposing three sememe-encoded models (Simple Sememe Aggregation SSA, Sememe Attention over Context SAC, Sememe Attention over Target SAT) that learn sememe, sense and word embeddings and use attention to select senses in context.
datasets: Chinese Word Analogy dataset (Chen et al., 2015)
limitations: For simplicity, the internal hierarchical/relational structure among sememes is ignored; each sense is represented by an unordered sememe set.
Relies on HowNet's assumption that the limited annotated sememes adequately represent real-world senses/words.

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a PhD in computational linguistics who has led reproducible evaluation suites for word representation research and implemented many embedding models in production.
traits: focuses on experimental rigor: checks for proper baselines, statistical significance, and evaluation protocols
values reproducibility: looks for code/data release, hyperparameters, and training details to re-run results
insists on ablation studies and controlled comparisons to isolate the contribution of each model component
notices optimization/training issues: initialization, negative sampling, convergence criteria, and computational cost
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Practical, detail-oriented reviewer who will verify that experiments are sound, sufficiently ablated, and reproducible and will request clarifications or extra experiments where necessary.

reviewer_b.name: Lexical Semantics & Resources Expert
reviewer_b.background: Professor in NLP with extensive publications on lexical semantics, lexical resources (e.g., HowNet, WordNet) and sense-aware representations, and experience evaluating interpretability and real-world impact of linguistic resources.
traits: evaluates novelty and positioning relative to prior sense/sememe/sense-annotated embedding work
values theoretical and practical significance: how the approach advances understanding of semantics and utility for downstream tasks
attends to resource limitations and generality: coverage of HowNet, language/domain dependence, and scalability
notices explanatory value: whether attention/sememe selection genuinely yields interpretable sense decisions
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Contextual, big-picture reviewer who assesses novelty, relevance to the field, and whether the proposed use of sememes meaningfully advances lexical representation and interpretability.
